text: In late October, a winter storm dumped heavy snow in eastern North Dakota and northwestern Minnesota over a period of 2 days. Due to mesoscale forces at play, some areas received a foot of snow or more where the band stalled. In addition to heavy snow, there was also blowing snow, reducing visibility across the area. Public reports 7.5 inches at Black Tiger Bay Campground in Saint Michael. Local Police Department relays storm total snow measurement of 12 inches in New Rockford. Public reports 10 inches of storm total snowfall 1 W Petersburg. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

An area of of resistant aufeis in the main channel of the Sagavanirktok (Sag) River during spring breakup forced the Sag River flow toward Milepost 403 of the Dalton Highway. The high flow from snowmelt and a heavy rain on snow event in the Brooks Range on June 10th exacerbated the impacts to the Dalton Highway. The main flow of the Sagavanirktok (Sag) River was diverted to the farthest west channel of the river due to aurfeis blocking the channel. The Sag River was pushed up against the Dalton Highway and began to erode the road near Milepost 403.5. On June 7th the road was closed at Milepost 403.5 due to the Sag River eroding the highway to the centerline with the threat of a total washout. This... Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

D2 Drought continued from September into early October in one county before subsiding in the middle of October. D2 drought indicated in Southern Clearwater from October 1st through the 17th, 2023. D2 drought indicated in Northern Clearwater from October 1st through the 17th, 2023. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A vigorous short-wave trough interacted with an approaching low pressure system and associated warm frontal boundary to trigger clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms across west-central Illinois during the late afternoon of August 6th. Due to enhanced low-level wind shear along and north of the warm front, many of the cells began rotating and spinning up occasional funnel clouds. One of the cells intensified as it pushed into Sangamon County, producing a long-track tornado that was on the ground for over 25 miles from just north of Pawnee in far southeastern Sangamon County to southeast of Stonington in Christian County. The tornado produced EF-1 damage along much of its path, peaking with EF-2 damage just south of Willeys in Christian County. This particular thunderstorm cell dropped 2-inch diameter hail north of Taylorville and went on to produce a downburst with 80 mph wind gusts in far eastern Shelby County near Lake Mattoon. The storms eventually congealed into a line as they pushed into eastern Illinois later in the evening, with scattered wind damage, hail, and flash flooding. A trained spotter estimated a 60 mph wind gust. An emergency manager estimated sustained winds of 50 mph with gusts to around 60 mph. Several 2 to 4-inch diameter tree branches were blown down. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a frontal boundary produced damaging winds across portions of central and southeast Virginia. Power line was downed on Seacock Chapel Road. Trees were downed near the Holland Bypass on Route 58. Tree was downed on the road near the intersection of Redhouse Road and Courthouse Highway. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Strong and gusty north to northeast winds impacted portions of Southwestern California. Widespread wind gusts in the 60 to 70 MPH range were reported with even some localized gusts over 100 MPH. Strong and gusty north to northeast winds impacted the southeastern valleys of Ventura county. Some peak wind gusts include: Miller Ranch (gust 65 MPH) and East Simi Valley Cochrane (gust 60 MPH). Strong and gusty north to northeast winds impacted the eastern range of the Santa Monica Mountains. Some peak wind gusts include: Malibu Hills RAWS (gust 64 MPH), Saddle Peak (gust 62 MPH) and Backbone Trail (gust 60 MPH). Strong and gusty north to northeast winds impacted the eastern range of the Santa Ynez Mountains. The Montecito Hills RAWS sensor reported wind gusts up to 68 MPH. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

The combined effects of persistent above-normal temperatures and minimal precipitation starting in early July 2023 resulted in mounting drought impacts across much of the state of Mississippi. Through the month of October, D2 (Severe Drought) and D3 (Extreme Drought) conditions, as depicted on the U.S. Drought Monitor, expanded from southern and central areas to also affect northern Mississippi. D4 (Exceptional Drought) conditions expanded farther across central and southwestern portions of the state including most areas encompassed by a loop from Vicksburg to Kosciusko to Philadelphia to Columbia. Average temperatures for the month especially in the most impacted areas ranged from 2 to 4 degrees F above normal, and several daily temperature records were set. Observed precipitation values across a vast majority of the state were less than 25 percent of normal. In southeastern Mississippi where around 2 to 3 inches of rain fell during the month - about 70 percent of normal - average temperatures were about 1 degree below normal. Soil moisture values continued to worsen through the month, with subsoil moisture values between 25 to 50 percent below normal for much of the affected... Drought conditions in Montgomery County intensified from D2 (Severe Drought) to D4 (Exceptional Drought) as depicted on the U.S. Drought Monitor. Drought conditions in Grenada County intensified from D2 (Severe Drought) to D4 (Exceptional Drought) as depicted on the U.S. Drought Monitor. Drought conditions in Washington County intensified from D1 (Moderate Drought) to D3 (Extreme Drought) as depicted on the U.S. Drought Monitor. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Thunderstorms developed near a warm front across central and east-central Wisconsin during the mid to late morning hours on Tuesday (10/24). The storms lifted north across north-central and northeast Wisconsin during the early afternoon hours. The strongest storms produced large hail up to 3 inches in diameter. Quarter size hail fell near Hewitt. Nickel size hail fell near Knowlton. Golf ball size hail fell in Knowlton. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A cold front triggered a broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms across southeast Illinois during the late afternoon of August 5th. One of the cells produced a brief tornado touchdown in rural Jasper County about 3 miles northeast of Willow Hill. No damage was reported. A tornado briefly touched down in an open field just south of Mound Church about 3 miles northeast of Willow Hill at 5:30 PM CDT. It quickly dissipated about 1 minute later, with no damage being reported. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Widespread severe weather was observed across Eastern Nebraska on the afternoon and evening of July 4. Storms initially were discrete or multi-clustered and produced mainly hail. The largest hail report was 2.75 inches near Tarnov in Platte County. A QLCS formed as storms merged along a cold front and strong wind gusts became the primary hazard. Numerous locations observed 60+ mph wind gusts. An estimated peak wind gust of 80 mph caused damage to trees and power poles near Weston. Public reported a large tree snapped off via social media. Trained spotter reported an estimated 60 mph wind gust. Trained spotter reported 1-inch hail. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A stalled frontal boundary remained over the area which produced multiple rounds of torrential rainfall form heavy thunderstorms from July 9th through July 10th. CAPE values of 2500 to 3500 J/kg and PWAT values of near 1.8 allowed heavy thunderstorm development to produce rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times. There were parts of the area that receive anywhere from 3-5 inches of rain in a several hour period with some spots seeing upwards of 8 inches. This allowed for the issuance of a localized Flash Flood Emergency for portion of the Lower Hudson Valley. Video of water rushing down Wixon Pond Road and East Lake Blvd in Carmel, NY. DOT reports flooding on US 202 both directions at Yorktown. All lanes blocked and local car dealership parking lot flooded. Video shows Rt 218 between Cornwall and West Point washed away. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Thunderstorms developed along an old outflow boundary across parts of central and east central Missouri. Some of the storms produced damaging winds. Widespread wind damage across Farmington. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. Also, a couple of power poles were knocked down. Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines on Highway Z about 2 miles south of Murry. In Columbia, Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. On tree fell onto a house near the intersection of Ash Street and Greenwood causing moderate roof damage. Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees on the east side of Sullivan. One tree fell onto Highway 185 near entrance to Meramec State Park. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A widespread significant damaging wind storm tracked across nearly all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the morning of July 12. These storms produced widespread winds of 60 to 80 mph, with embedded stronger downbursts with wind speeds up to 100 mph. The strongest measured wind gust was 82 mph in Dodge County NE, but some of the high-end straight line wind damage supports embedded wind speeds up to 100 mph.||This storm also produced a few embedded tornadoes. Some of the tornado tracks were only identified by narrow swaths of damage through mature cornfields, with minimal damage otherwise and peak wind speeds similar to the nearby downburst winds. All but one of the tornadoes was preceded by a Wireless Emergency Alert. There were 0 reported injuries or fatalities. Public reported a peak measured wind gust of 74 mph. Public reported a measured peak wind gust of 64 mph. Public reported large tree branches down. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A front produced heavy snow for the Oregon Cascades. Cold Air in the Columbia Gorge resulted in heavy snow as a front associated with an atmospheric river produced precipitation across NW Oregon. Trained Skywarn Spotters reported 4 to 6.5 inches of snow in Hood River at elevations 200 feet and below near Highway 84 in the morning of December 1. Widespread 1 to 2 feet snow totals were observed across the Cascades above 3000 feet. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A strong front produced hevy snow for the Southwest Washington Cascades. Cold Air in the Columbia Gorge resulted in heavy snow as a front associated with an atmospheric river produced precipitation across Southwest Washington. Washington Department of Transportation closed Highway 14 through the Columbia River Gorge between mileposts 18 and 43 due to the snow causing multiple vehicle spinouts. Trained Spotters reported 5 to 8 inches of snow around Skamania near Highway 14 the morning of December 1. Widespread 1-2 foot snow totals were observed across the South Washington Cascades for elevations above 3000 feet. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.
A strong front produced heavy snow for the Oregon Cascades. A quick 1 to 2 feet of snow was observed above 4000 feet within a 12 hour period. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

The Upper Hood River appeared to have received 0.50 to 1.00 inch of ice. Freezing rain occurred in the Upper Hood River Valley and appeared to have received 0.50 to 1.00 inch of ice based on reports from heated gages such as Parkdale. LSR report of 0.25 inch ice near Odell. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A strong front resulted in high winds along the Southwest Washington Coast on December 2, 2023. The Oregon Department of Transportation Wind sensor located on the Megler Bridge between the Oregon and Washington in Astoria, Oregon reported a peak wind of 55 mph with a gust of 70 mph. A wind sensor at Cape Disappointment measured wind gusts of 65 mph. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Freezing rain in the South Washington Cascade foothills. The Wind River Valley appears to have received 0.50 to 1.00 inch of ice. Freezing rain occurred in the south Washington Cascade foothills, including the Wind River Valley, which appears to have received 0.50 to 1.00 inch of ice. A trained spotter measured 0.10 inch of ice at 1300 ft elevation near Underwood. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A cold front associated with an occluding surface low over the upper Midwest pushed through the waters in the early morning hours of January 4th. Along the front, a line of thunderstorms formed and pushed through the waters, bringing winds of over 34 knots. The cold front, and subsequent thunderstorms, exited the outer waters by 12Z. Mesonet station XMVL recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 43 mph or 37 knots. Mesonet station at mid bridge Lake Pontchartrain recorded a wind gust of 43 mph or 37 knots. NDBC buoy 42040 measured a peak wind gust of 42 mph or 37 knots from thunderstorm winds. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A pair of moderate atmospheric rivers brought heavy rain leading to minor river and urban flooding to portions of northwest Oregon. The Siuslaw river at Mapleton rose above minor flood stage after noon on December 3rd and crested at 18.7 feet at 6 PM. Flood stage is 18.0 feet. A man fell into Johnson Creek and drowned. Moderate to heavy rainfall December 2nd through 3rd produced bank full conditions and swift moving water in the creek. Spotter reported a rockslide on Highway 36, with small trees down, but could move around them. Tripcheck showed rockslide between Rainrock and Swisshome, OR. Areas of running water over Highway 36 and the train tracks was also observed. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Winds impacts for metro and columbia river gorge including power outages. Mesonet stations measured gusts of 45 to 50 mph. Multiple reports of fallen trees on power lines. Hundreds of PGE customers without power in Clackamas, Washington and Multnomah Counties. High winds in the Columbia River Gorge. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A strong front produced a burst of high winds along the North Oregon Coast on December 4, 2023. Several wind measuring sites measured high winds along the Oregon coast December 4th through December 5th. The peak measured wind was 71 mph at Garibaldi. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A strong atmospheric river brought heavy rains on December 5th which led to minor river and urban flooding across portions of southwest Washington. Clark County and Cowlitz County saw landslides, along with minor river flooding, including the Cowlitz River at Kelso and the Grays River near Rosburg. Portions of the Willapa Hills and south Washington Cascades received 12 to 16 inches of rain over a 5 day period beginning December 1st. The AMTRAK rail experienced a small landslide on their track between mile marker 129 and 130.5 near Felida and canceled the train between Seattle and Portland through December 7th. No impacts other than their internal transportation delays occurred. The Grays River rose above flood stage on December 4th, cresting at 16.24 feet on December 5th at 9 am, and fell below flood stage on December 6th. Flood stage is 12.0 feet. The Willapa River at Willapa rose reached minor flood stage during a period of 1 and half hours, cresting at 21.03 feet at 315 pm on December 5th. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A strong upper level trough ejected over the Southern Plains which caused rapid surface cyclogenesis to occur in southeast Texas. This surface low traversed across central and northern Louisiana. The rapid development of the surface low gave way to very high shear values, and with enough instability, a line of thunderstorms formed along its cold front. A couple of brief tornadoes occurred with the line in Pointe Coupee and Terrebonne Parishes. The Pointe Coupee Parish tornado resulted in 3 injuries. Otherwise, damaging wind gusts were noted with the line due to the extreme shear. The line exited southeast Louisiana in the early morning hours of the 25th. Emergency manager reported a travel trailer rolled and was destroyed on Stewart Street. Time estimated from radar. Large tree down on Brewster Road near Highway 1077. Time estimated from radar. Trained spotter measured 65 mph wind gust using handheld anemometer. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Dense Fog in the Central Willamette Valley contributed to a aircraft accident. A small plane struck power pole and power lines near Independence, Oregon and crashed just outside airport grounds, killing all three people onboard. Dense Fog in the Central Willamette Valley contributed to deadly aircraft accident. A single engine airplane travelling from McMinnville to Independence crashed around 4:55 PM into a power pole just outside of the Independence Airport. The crash resulted in a local power outage, a small brushfire and the loss of all three people on board the airplane. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A southwesterly swell of 20 to 25 ft with a period of 17 seconds was observed across the coastal waters, resulting in a high threat of sneaker waves. A southwesterly swell of 20 to 25 ft with a period of 17 seconds was observed across the coastal waters. A 5 year old girl was hospitalized after she was hit by a sneaker wave near South Jetty Beach in Florence, OR. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A moderate winter storm impacted Southwestern California early in the month. Rainfall totals ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across coastal and valley areas with 1 to 3 inches across the mountains. Cold air, associated with the storm, dropped snow levels to around 3000 feet. So, there were significant snowfall accumulations in the local mountains. Scattered thunderstorms also developed, producing some wind damage. Strong and gusty northerly winds impacted the eastern range of the Santa Ynez Mountains. Some peak gusts from the local mesonet include: Montecito Hills (gust 71 MPH) and La Granada Mountain (gust 66 MPH). Winter storm conditions developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. Snowfall amounts above 5000 foot elevation ranged between 8 and 20 inches. Along with the snowfall, strong winds were observed. Some peak gusts from the local mesonet include: Magic Mountain Truck Trail (gust 64 MPH) and Ford Observatory (gust 64 MPH). Winter storm conditions developed across the mountains of Ventura county. Snowfall amounts above 5000 foot elevation ranged between 8 and 20 inches. Along with the snowfall, strong winds were observed with gusts around 60 MPH reported. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

An Enhanced Risk was issued for severe weather on May 7. A shortwave moving across the area interacted with a couple of surface boundaries in the area initiating severe weather. There was video of a weak tornado that didn't produce any damage. There were several hail reports the largest of which was 2.25 inches in Mills County. Public reported 1-inch hail. Trained spotter reported hail in varying sizes ranging from 1.75 inches up to 2.25 inches. Public sent a video of a brief tornado kicking up a bit of dust resulting in no damage. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Another strong winter storm impacted Southwestern California. The storm brought winter storm conditions the local mountains with snowfall totals of 2 to 4 feet and southerly winds gusting in excess of 80 MPH. Other areas also experienced some strong winds. Additionally, scattered thunderstorms developed across the area, spawning two weak tornadoes. Strong southerly winds impacted the interior valleys of San Luis Obispo county. Some peak wind gusts from the local mesonet include: Navajo Flats (gust 59 MPH) and Lewis Ranch Road (gust 58 MPH). Winter storm conditions developed across the Interstate 5 Corridor in the Los Angeles county mountains. Snowfall totals of 1 to 3 feet were reported above 6000 feet elevation with 10 to 20 inches between 5000 and 6000 feet. Additionally, there were some very strong southerly winds. Some peak gusts from the local mesonet include: Whitaker Peak (gust 54 MPH) and Sandberg ASOS (gust 53 MPH). Winter storm conditions developed across the northern Ventura county mountains. Snowfall totals of 1 to 3 feet were reported above 6000 feet elevation with 8 to 16 inches between 5000 and 6000 feet. Additionally, there were some very strong southerly winds with gusts in excess of 50 MPH reported. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

One last Winter storm impacted Southwestern California in the month of March. Rainfall totals across the area ranged between 1 and 3 inches which produced numerous reports of nuisance urban flooding. In the mountains of Los Angeles county, snowfall totals up to 12 inches, combined with wind gusting to 67 MPH, generated winter storm conditions. Winter storm conditions developed across the eastern range of the San Gabriel Mountains. Above 5000 feet, snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were reported. Additionally, southerly winds, gusting up to 67 MPH, were reported. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

An Enhanced Risk was issued for severe weather on May 7. A shortwave moving across the area interacted with a couple of surface boundaries in the area initiating severe weather. There were numerous hail and wind reports across the area. The largest hail report was 2.5 inches in Lancaster County and the highest measured wind gust was 73 mph in Richardson County. Public reported 1-inch hail via mPing. Emergency Manager reported 1.75-inch hail. Received a report for 1-inch hail which was relayed via an amateur radio. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A front produced high winds along the northwest Oregon coast December 2, 2023. The Oregon Department of Transportation wind sensor on Megler Bridge measured a peak gust of 70 mph. A wind sensor at Clatsop Spit measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Eastern Nebraska was in a Marginal Risk for severe weather as instability increased due to strong moisture advection ahead of a Colorado low. The first round was on the evening of May 6 as a severe storm developed in Jefferson and Saline counties producing a couple of hail reports. After a few hours, storms redeveloped in northeast Nebraska producing a few rounds of hail with the largest being ping pong ball sized hail in Antelope County. Fire Department reported 1-inch hail. Public report 1-inch hail via mPing. Public reported multiple rounds of hail with the largest being ping pong ball sized hail. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A strong low-pressure system which had been moving slowly off the lee side of the Rocky Mountains had finally progressed into central Nebraska. This left western Iowa in the warm sector as a warm front moved north into the area. The SPC issued an Enhanced Risk for severe weather with a 30% hatched for severe hail. The severe weather mostly stayed west of the Missouri River. In Iowa, there was one report for a severe wind gust. The most impactful weather was a line of training storms that lead to flash flooding in Harrison County that caused significant damage. Public reported 4.4 inches of rainfall. Public reported via mPing of 1-inch tree limbs broken; shingles blown off. Forest Service reported up to two dozen culverts washed out and six county roads that were partially or fully washed out. Large trees were washed into fields and there was significant damage to farm fields in several areas in the counties. There are significant areas of crop fields that likely lost in excess of 100 tons per acre from that one heavy rain event. The county estimated the damage... Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

An upper level low crossed the region during the 5th. Instability due to diurnal heating and the cold pool aloft supported the development of thunderstorms during the afternoon. Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms occurred across portions of Downeast Maine with hail and damaging winds. Three trees blown down near Cooper, Maine. Two people were struck by lightning and transported to the hospital. The extent of the injuries is uncertain. The time is estimated. Several trees were toppled by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph in Marion Township. The time is estimated. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

High pressure at the surface and aloft brought hot temperatures and high dew points to the region during the 6th. Afternoon temperatures ranged from the mid 80s to around 90...with dew points from the upper 60s to lower 70s...across much of northern and eastern Maine. Heat Index values generally ranged from the mid 90s to around 100 degrees...locally up to 105. High temperatures ranged from the mid 80s to around 90...with dew points from the upper 60s to lower 70s. This combination produced Heat Index values generally ranging from the mid 90s to around 100 degrees...locally up to around 105. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

On May 16, 2023, a 1002 mb low moving through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to the Bering Strait. Blizzard conditions developed on the Bering Strait coast early in the morning, of the 16th and continued into the early evening. Blizzard conditions were reported at both Brevig Mission and Wales. Brevig Mission had two distinct periods of blizzard conditions during this event, with blizzard conditions present for much of the morning, with a second shorter period in the evening. Wales reported... Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Scattered late afternoon storms produced damaging winds across southeast Arkansas. Several trees were blown down. Multiple trees and powerlines were blown down in town. Several trees were down across roads as well. A tree was blown down across Highway 52. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A weather front brought from 1 to 2 inches of rain to the headwaters of Birch Creek and Crooked Creek. The rain fell on a residual winter snowpack and resulted in significant runoff due to the combination of rapid snowmelt and the heavy rainfall. This caused high water and flooding along rivers and streams draining the region including Crooked Creek and Birch Creek which impacted the community of Central and portions of the Steese Highway. Flooding started in late May and continued into early June. Heavy rain and snowmelt in the Birch Creek headwaters caused Birch Creek to flow overbank and flood portions of the Steese Highway between milepost 145 to 147. The flooding caused the highway to be closed for a period of time due to dangerous driving conditions. Birch Creek fell back within bank on June 2nd. Heavy rain on snow caused Crooked Creek in Central to rise overbank and flood portions of the community. The public reported flood waters up to the doors of the Central Museum, into the parking lot of the local store, and up to 6 inches deep over the Steese Highway in Central. Residents put sandbags around the Central Museum but water still seeped in through the back door and got the carpet... Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A weak upper-level wave moving through southwest flow aloft, acting on an environment with 1000 to 1500 J/kg of MUCAPE in the vicinity of a surface trough, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota during the evening of April 19th. The strongest storms produced severe hail ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter. Hail 1.0 inch in diameter fell 5 miles west-northwest of Sioux City. Hail 1.0 inch in diameter fell in Rock Valley. Hail 1.5 inches in diameter fell in Doon. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Most of the region was in Extreme (D3) to Exceptional (D4) Drought for entire month of September. The county was in Extreme (D3) to Exceptional (D4) Drought for entire month of September due a long duration lack of rainfall and a very hot, dry period. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Scattered afternoon storms developed each day and some reached strong to severe limits. A tree was blown down onto a powerline along Woody Drive. Numerous trees were blown down in the county. A tree was blown down across East Railroad road. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.
A cluster of storms moved up from the southwest during the afternoon and produced wind damage. Numerous trees and powerlines were blown down. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

The U.S. Drought Monitor designated Extreme Drought (D3) conditions for all counties in Northern Alabama as of November 2nd. This issuance now formally includes Limestone, Lawrence, and Cullman Counties.||With the next issuance on the 9th, Exceptional (D4) Conditions were introduced into Jackson and Dekalb Counties, which expanded with the issuance on the 16th. Additional counties included are Madison, Marshall, Morgan, Lauderdale, Colbert, and Franklin Counties in AL. At this time, D3 expanded and covered all remaining portions of the County Warning Area (CWA). Little/No change was made for the issuance on the 22nd. ||Exceptional (D4) Drought Conditions were removed after above normal rainfall began for the latter part of November. No counties were included in D4; all of the CWA was included in D3 conditions to end the month. The U.S. Drought Monitor designated Extreme Drought (D3) conditions for all counties in Northern Alabama as of November 2nd. This issuance now formally includes Limestone, Lawrence, and Cullman Counties.||With the next issuance on the 9th, Exceptional (D4) Conditions were introduced into Jackson and Dekalb Counties, which expanded with the issuance on the 16th. Additional counties included are... Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Another unseasonably strong upper trough approached the region during the day on the 17th. Widespread thunderstorm development occurred across the High Plains, from southwest Kansas into the central Texas Panhandle by the late afternoon. The combination of anomalously (for mid-June) high magnitudes of wind shear and instability produced widespread severe weather outcomes across southern, central and northern Oklahoma. By the late evening hours, convection had organized into a mature bow echo across north-central Oklahoma, where numerous significant wind gusts (and damage) were observed. Similar outcomes occurred into portions of northeastern Oklahoma towards early morning hours of the 18th. A tornado developed and produced a Tornadic Debris Signature (TDS) on the KVNX radar north of Burlington. The tornado moved primarily over cropland, although it did apparently damage a few trees. Numerous power lines blown down and a tree blocking the road in the community of Wellston. Multiple power poles blown down and roof damage to several buildings in the community of Cushing. Time is radar estimated. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

Drier air began to work into eastern Utah in the wake of a recent monsoon surge. Even so, enough residual moisture was present to fuel isolated thunderstorms. Some storms produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph in Grand County. A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport outside of Moab. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A stalled frontal boundary just north of southwest New Hampshire allowed for this region of the state to stay in a moist and unstable airmass on the afternoon of June 26th. Thunderstorms begin to develop during the afternoon hours and continued into the evening. Most storms remained on the week, except for one isolated storm along the Connecticut River Valley in Cheshire County which became severe. Severe thunderstorm outflow winds downed multiple trees onto buildings in the Spofford Lake area. Severe thunderstorm outflow winds downed multiple trees on Maine Street and Pine Street in Swanzey. A server thunderstorm dropped hail up to the size of quarters in Chesterfield. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A stationary front draped over the region provided the focus for slow moving thunderstorm activity over northeastern Pennsylvania. Localized areas of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall contributed to some small stream and creek flooding near the Delaware River. The public reported water, rocks and gravel running across Twin Lakes Road near the Handsome Eddy Road intersection due to culvert being blown out by flood waters. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.

A powerful storm system, with highly anomalous magnitudes of wind shear and moisture for late February, impacted the forecast area on the evening of the 26th. A broken line of severe thunderstorms developed across the eastern Texas Panhandle/far western Oklahoma and quickly swept through much of the forecast area. Widespread wind damage, including gusts near 90 mph, along with a record number of tornadoes (13) for February occurred. Several reports of large hail were also received. Behind the line of thunderstorms, a tight pressure gradient and strong westerly low-level jet allowed widespread 50-60 mph winds gusts to continue through the late evening hours. Time is estimated via radar. A few homes suffered roof damage, barns were damaged, and an RV was flipped over along Norman Road in Enid from thunderstorm winds. Homes and barns were damaged about 1.5 to 2 miles north of Fort Cobb Lake Dam. Daily impact metrics for this episode: <ts></ts>.
